Exception hierarchy + Abstract service — Spring Boot super patterns 
// ── Custom exception using super() ──────────────────────────
public class AppException extends RuntimeException {
    private final int httpStatus;

    public AppException(String message, int httpStatus) {
        super(message);        // passes message to RuntimeException
        this.httpStatus = httpStatus;
    }
    public int getHttpStatus() { return httpStatus; }
}

public class ResourceNotFoundException extends AppException {
    public ResourceNotFoundException(String resource, Long id) {
        super(resource + " not found with id: " + id, 404);
    }
}

// ── Abstract base service — super.method() to reuse logic ────
public abstract class BaseService<T> {
    protected void validateNotNull(T entity) {
        if (entity == null)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Entity must not be null");
    }
}

@Service
public class UserService extends BaseService<User> {
    public void save(User user) {
        super.validateNotNull(user);  // reuse parent validation
        // ... additional UserService-specific logic
    }
}
📌 Exception chaining with super(message, cause)
When wrapping a lower-level exception in a custom one, pass the original exception as the cause:
super(message, originalException)
This preserves the full stack trace, making debugging much easier. Spring's @ControllerAdvice can then unwrap and log it properly.
⭐ Must-Know Exam Points
  • super has three uses: access field, call method, call constructor
  • super() must be the first statement in a constructor
  • this() and super() cannot both appear in the same constructor
  • If no super() or this() written, compiler inserts super() automatically
  • If parent has no no-arg constructor → implicit super() fails → compile error
  • Constructor bodies execute parent first, child last (top-down)
  • super cannot be used in a static context
  • Every chain eventually reaches Object()

📝 MCQ Traps

QuestionAnswerTrap / Reason
Where must super() appear in a constructor?First statementAny other position is a compile error
Can you use super() and this() together?No — in same constructorBoth require first-statement position; only one can be first
What does compiler insert if no super() or this()?super() — no-argAlways inserted; fails if parent has no no-arg constructor
In what order do constructor bodies run?Parent first, child lastObject runs first, deepest child runs last
Can super be used in a static method?Nosuper needs this reference — unavailable in static context
super.method() — which method does it call?Immediate parent's versionBypasses dynamic dispatch; not necessarily grandparent

📖 One-Liner Definitions

  • super keyword — Reference to the immediate parent class; used to access hidden fields, call overridden methods, or invoke parent constructors.
  • super() — Calls the parent class constructor; must be the first statement in a child constructor; implicitly inserted by compiler if omitted.
  • Constructor chaining — A sequence of constructor calls (via this() or super()) that propagates up the inheritance hierarchy to Object.
  • Implicit super() — The no-arg parent constructor call inserted automatically by the compiler when no explicit super() or this() is written.
  • super.method() — Directly invokes the parent class's version of an overridden method, bypassing runtime polymorphism.
TOPIC 16

Method Overriding

🔄 What is Method Overriding?

Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides its own implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass, using the same name, same parameter list, and same (or covariant) return type. The subclass version replaces the parent version for objects of that subclass.

Overriding is the mechanism behind runtime polymorphism — the JVM decides at runtime which version to call based on the actual type of the object, not the reference type.

METHOD OVERRIDING — BASIC STRUCTURE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("Some sound"); } } class Dog extends Animal { @Override // annotation — optional but strongly recommended void sound() { System.out.println("Woof!"); } } class Cat extends Animal { @Override void sound() { System.out.println("Meow!"); } } Animal a = new Dog(); // parent reference, child object a.sound(); // Output: Woof! ← Dog's version called // Decided at RUNTIME — not compile time

📋 Rules for Method Overriding

RuleDetail
Same method nameExact same name as in parent
Same parameter listSame number, types, and order of parameters (different = overloading, not overriding)
Same or covariant return typeReturn type must be same or a subtype of the parent's return type
Access modifierMust be same or wider — can widen (protected → public) but NOT narrow (public → private)
Checked exceptionsCan throw fewer or narrower checked exceptions — cannot throw new or broader ones
IS-A requiredOnly possible between parent and child class (inheritance required)

❌ What CANNOT Be Overridden

CANNOT OVERRIDE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1. static methods → they are hidden, not overridden (method hiding — resolved at compile time) 2. private methods → not inherited; child can define same name but it is a new method, not an override 3. final methods → compiler enforces — compile error if attempted 4. Constructors → constructors are never inherited or overridden Example of method HIDING (static): class A { static void show() { print("A"); } } class B extends A { static void show() { print("B"); } } A obj = new B(); obj.show(); // prints "A" ← resolved at compile time via reference type // NOT "B" — this is NOT polymorphism!

🔬 Overriding vs Overloading — Side by Side

OverridingOverloading
WhereSubclass overrides parent methodSame class (or subclass), different signature
SignatureSame name + same paramsSame name + different params
Return typeSame or covariantCan be anything (doesn't affect resolution)
Resolved atRuntime (dynamic dispatch)Compile time (static dispatch)
PolymorphismRuntime polymorphismCompile-time polymorphism
@OverrideApplicable and recommendedNot applicable
InheritanceRequiredNot required

📌 @Override Annotation

The @Override annotation tells the compiler: "I intend this method to override a parent method." If the signature doesn't match any parent method, the compiler gives an error — catching typos and signature mismatches before runtime. Always use it.

⚠️ Forgetting @Override silently creates a new method instead
If you misspell the method name (e.g., tostring() instead of toString()) without @Override, Java creates a brand new method. Your override never happens — the parent's version is still called. @Override would have caught this at compile time.

🔁 Covariant Return Type

Since Java 5, an overriding method can return a subtype of the parent method's return type. This is called a covariant return type.

COVARIANT RETURN TYPE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── class Animal { Animal create() { return new Animal(); } } class Dog extends Animal { @Override Dog create() { return new Dog(); } // Dog IS-A Animal → valid! } // Parent returns Animal, child returns Dog (a subtype) — OK

💻 Program 1 — Method Overriding & Runtime Polymorphism

MethodOverriding.java 
class Shape {
    String color;

    Shape(String color) { this.color = color; }

    double area() {
        return 0;  // default — overridden by each shape
    }

    void describe() {
        System.out.printf("%-12s | color: %-6s | area: %.2f%n",
            getClass().getSimpleName(), color, area());
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    Circle(String color, double radius) {
        super(color); this.radius = radius;
    }
    @Override
    double area() { return Math.PI * radius * radius; }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double width, height;
    Rectangle(String color, double w, double h) {
        super(color); width = w; height = h;
    }
    @Override
    double area() { return width * height; }
}

class Triangle extends Shape {
    double base, heightT;
    Triangle(String color, double b, double h) {
        super(color); base = b; heightT = h;
    }
    @Override
    double area() { return 0.5 * base * heightT; }
}

public class MethodOverriding {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Parent reference array — polymorphic behaviour
        Shape[] shapes = {
            new Circle("Red",    5),
            new Rectangle("Blue",  4, 6),
            new Triangle("Green", 3, 8),
            new Shape("None")     // base shape — area 0
        };
        for (Shape s : shapes)
            s.describe();   // calls each class's area() at runtime
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
Circle | color: Red | area: 78.54 Rectangle | color: Blue | area: 24.00 Triangle | color: Green | area: 12.00 Shape | color: None | area: 0.00

💻 Program 2 — Access Modifier Widening & Covariant Return

OverrideRules.java 
class Vehicle {
    // protected → child can widen to public, cannot narrow to private
    protected Vehicle getInstance() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle.getInstance()");
        return new Vehicle();
    }

    protected void fuel() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle uses fuel");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {

    @Override
    public Car getInstance() {          // ✅ wider (public) + covariant (Car)
        System.out.println("Car.getInstance() — covariant return");
        return new Car();
    }

    @Override
    public void fuel() {                 // ✅ widened: protected → public
        System.out.println("Car uses petrol");
    }

    // ❌ would be compile error:
    // @Override private void fuel() { }   narrowing public → private
}

public class OverrideRules {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle v = new Car();    // parent ref, child object
        v.getInstance();           // Car's version runs — runtime dispatch
        v.fuel();                   // Car's version runs

        Car c = new Car();
        Car ref = c.getInstance();  // covariant: returns Car directly (no cast)
        ref.fuel();
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
Car.getInstance() — covariant return Car uses petrol Car.getInstance() — covariant return Car uses petrol

💻 Program 3 — Method Hiding (static) vs Overriding

HidingVsOverriding.java 
class Parent {
    static void staticMethod() {
        System.out.println("Parent - static");
    }
    void instanceMethod() {
        System.out.println("Parent - instance");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    static void staticMethod() {     // METHOD HIDING — not overriding
        System.out.println("Child - static");
    }
    @Override
    void instanceMethod() {         // TRUE OVERRIDING
        System.out.println("Child - instance");
    }
}

public class HidingVsOverriding {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Parent p = new Child();

        // Static — resolved by REFERENCE TYPE (compile time)
        p.staticMethod();    // "Parent - static"  ← reference is Parent

        // Instance — resolved by OBJECT TYPE (runtime)
        p.instanceMethod();  // "Child - instance" ← object is Child
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
Parent - static Child - instance
⚠️ Static methods are hidden, not overridden — no polymorphism
Static methods belong to the class, not to any object. When you call a static method via a reference variable, Java uses the reference type (compile-time type) to resolve it — not the actual object type. This is method hiding, not overriding, and it does NOT participate in runtime polymorphism. @Override on a static method causes a compile error.
💡 Always use @Override — it costs nothing and saves hours
@Override is free insurance. It catches: misspelled method names, wrong parameter types, trying to override a final or static method, and trying to override when there is no parent method to override. Make it a habit to always add it when intending to override.
⭐ Must-Know Interview Topics
Overriding rules (name/params/return/access/exceptions) · what cannot be overridden · @Override purpose · method hiding vs overriding · covariant return · overriding vs overloading · runtime polymorphism mechanism
Q1. What are the rules for method overriding in Java?
1. Same method name — exact match
2. Same parameter list — same types, count, and order (different params = overloading)
3. Same or covariant return type — return type must be the same or a subtype of the parent's return type
4. Access modifier must be same or wider — can widen (protected → public) but cannot narrow (public → private/protected)
5. Checked exceptions must be same or narrower — cannot declare new or broader checked exceptions
6. Inheritance required — can only override a method from a superclass
Q2. What methods cannot be overridden?
final methods — compiler error if you try; sealed by the parent
static methods — they are hidden, not overridden; resolved by reference type, not object type
private methods — not inherited at all; a child defining the same name creates a brand new independent method
Constructors — constructors are never inherited and cannot be overridden
Q3. What is the purpose of @Override annotation?
@Override tells the compiler: "I intend this to override a parent method." The compiler then verifies the contract:
— If no matching parent method exists → compile error (catches typos, wrong params)
— If trying to override a final or static method → compile error

Without @Override, a typo like tostring() instead of toString() silently creates a new independent method. The parent's toString() is never overridden and incorrect output appears at runtime with no error. @Override is optional but universally considered mandatory in professional code.
Q4. What is the difference between method overriding and method hiding?
Method overriding (instance methods): the child's version replaces the parent's at runtime. Which version runs is determined by the actual object type — this is runtime polymorphism / dynamic dispatch.

Method hiding (static methods): defining a static method in a child with the same name as a parent static method does not override it. Which version runs is determined by the reference type at compile time. No runtime polymorphism occurs. A Parent reference always calls the Parent static method even if the object is a Child.
Q5. What is a covariant return type?
A covariant return type means the overriding method can return a subtype of the parent method's declared return type.

Example: if the parent declares Animal getInstance(), the child can override it as Dog getInstance() since Dog is a subtype of Animal. This is valid since Java 5.

Benefit: callers using a Dog reference get back a Dog directly without casting; callers using an Animal reference still work because a Dog IS-A Animal. Factory and builder patterns use this extensively.
Q6. Can you override a method and make it throw a broader checked exception?
No — the overriding method cannot throw new or broader checked exceptions than the parent.

If the parent declares void read() throws IOException, the child can:
— throw FileNotFoundException (subclass of IOException — narrower ✅)
— throw nothing at all ✅
— throw Exception (superclass of IOException — broader ❌ — compile error)

Reason: code holding a Parent reference only catches IOException. If the child threw Exception, that catch block would miss it — violating the Liskov Substitution Principle.
Note: unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException and subclasses) have no such restriction — they can be freely added or broadened in overrides.
Q7. How does the JVM decide which overridden method to call?
Through Dynamic Method Dispatch (also called virtual method invocation). At runtime, the JVM looks at the actual type of the object on the heap, not the type of the reference variable, to determine which method to call.

Mechanism: each object carries a reference to its class's virtual method table (vtable), which maps method names to their most-derived implementation. When a method is called via a reference, the JVM follows the reference → object → vtable → most specific override.

This is why: Animal a = new Dog(); a.sound(); calls Dog.sound() — the vtable for the Dog object points to Dog.sound().

🌱 Method Overriding in Spring Boot

Overriding is fundamental to Spring Boot — the framework is designed around polymorphism. You override methods to customise framework behaviour, implement interfaces, and extend base classes.

✅ Common overriding patterns in Spring Boot
toString() / equals() / hashCode() — overriding Object methods on entities for logging and JPA identity
UserDetailsService.loadUserByUsername() — override to provide custom user lookup for Spring Security
WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter methods — override configure(HttpSecurity) to define security rules (legacy)
JpaRepository custom query methods — override default save/delete behaviour in repository
AbstractHealthIndicator.doHealthCheck() — override to add custom health checks to Actuator
CustomUserDetailsService.java + User.java — Spring Boot overriding 
// ── Override loadUserByUsername for Spring Security ──────────
@Service
public class CustomUserDetailsService implements UserDetailsService {

    private final UserRepository userRepo;

    public CustomUserDetailsService(UserRepository userRepo) {
        this.userRepo = userRepo;
    }

    @Override          // implements UserDetailsService contract
    public UserDetails loadUserByUsername(String username)
            throws UsernameNotFoundException {
        return userRepo.findByEmail(username)
            .orElseThrow(() ->
                new UsernameNotFoundException("User not found: " + username));
    }
}

// ── Override toString/equals/hashCode on JPA entity ──────────
@Entity
public class User {
    @Id @GeneratedValue
    private Long id;
    private String email;

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "User{id=" + id + ", email='" + email + "'}";
    }

    @Override   // JPA entities should use id for equality
    public boolean equals(Object o) {
        if (this == o) return true;
        if (!(o instanceof User)) return false;
        return id != null && id.equals(((User) o).id);
    }

    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return getClass().hashCode();
    }
}
📌 Why JPA entities need special equals/hashCode
Default Object.equals() uses memory address — two entities loaded at different times representing the same DB row would be unequal. Hibernate collections and caching rely on equals()/hashCode() for identity. Best practice: base equality on the database ID, and use getClass().hashCode() as a stable hash that works even before the ID is set.
⭐ Must-Know Exam Points
  • Overriding: same name + same params + same/covariant return + same/wider access
  • Cannot override: final, static, private methods, constructors
  • Static methods are hidden (compile-time), not overridden (runtime)
  • @Override — optional but causes compile error if no parent method matches
  • Access modifier: can widen (protected→public), cannot narrow (public→private)
  • Checked exceptions: can throw fewer/narrower, not more/broader
  • Covariant return type: child can return a subtype (since Java 5)
  • Runtime: resolved by object type; Static: resolved by reference type

📝 MCQ Traps

QuestionAnswerTrap / Reason
Can you override a private method?NoPrivate methods aren't inherited — child defines a new independent method
Can you override a static method?No — it is hiddenStatic methods use compile-time (reference type) dispatch, not runtime dispatch
Can overriding method narrow access (public→private)?No — compile errorMust be same or wider access modifier
Parent: void m() throws IOException. Child can throw…?Nothing, or FileNotFoundExceptionCannot throw Exception (broader) — compile error
Animal a = new Dog(); a.sound() calls…?Dog's sound()Runtime dispatch — object type (Dog) determines method
Parent a = new Child(); a.staticMethod() calls…?Parent's staticMethod()Static uses reference type — method hiding, not polymorphism
Is @Override mandatory for overriding?No — but strongly recommendedOptional, but without it typos create new methods silently

📖 One-Liner Definitions

  • Method overriding — A subclass providing its own implementation of an inherited method with the same signature; resolved at runtime.
  • Dynamic method dispatch — The JVM mechanism that resolves an overridden method call based on the actual runtime type of the object, not the reference type.
  • Method hiding — Redefining a static method in a subclass; resolved at compile time by reference type — not polymorphism.
  • Covariant return type — An overriding method returning a subtype of the parent method's return type; valid since Java 5.
  • @Override — Annotation that instructs the compiler to verify a method truly overrides a parent method; causes a compile error if the signature doesn't match.
TOPIC 17

Packages

📦 What is a Package?

A package is a namespace that groups related classes, interfaces, enums, and annotations together. On the file system, a package maps directly to a folder/directory. Package names follow the reverse domain name convention to guarantee global uniqueness.

📌 Two purposes of packages
1. Namespace management — two classes with the same name can coexist in different packages: com.app.util.Date vs java.util.Date
2. Access control — the default (package-private) access modifier restricts visibility to classes in the same package; packages form the boundary for that access level

📂 Package → Folder Mapping

PACKAGE NAME TO DIRECTORY STRUCTURE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Package declaration: com.example.service File system: src/ └── com/ └── example/ └── service/ └── OrderService.java First line of OrderService.java: package com.example.service; Rules: → Package name must match the directory path exactly → All lowercase, dot-separated → Reverse domain: com.google.*, org.apache.*, io.github.* → No hyphens (invalid Java identifier); underscores allowed but rare ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

🔑 The package Statement

The package statement must be the very first non-comment line in a Java source file. Only one package statement is allowed per file.

PACKAGE STATEMENT POSITION ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // optional: comments and blank lines allowed here package com.example.service; ← MUST be first statement import java.util.List; ← imports come after package import java.util.ArrayList; public class OrderService { } ← class definition last ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── If no package statement → class belongs to the "default package" (unnamed package). Avoid for anything beyond quick demos.

📥 The import Statement

To use a class from another package you either use its fully qualified name every time, or add an import statement so you can use the simple name.

IMPORT TYPES ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1. Single-type import (preferred): import java.util.ArrayList; import java.util.List; // uses only what you name — clear and explicit 2. Wildcard import (use with care): import java.util.*; // imports ALL public types from java.util // does NOT import sub-packages (java.util.concurrent.* is separate) // does NOT slow compilation — only resolution at compile time 3. Static import (for static members): import static java.lang.Math.PI; import static java.lang.Math.*; // allows: area = PI * r * r instead of Math.PI * r * r 4. No import needed: → java.lang.* (String, System, Math, Object...) — auto-imported → Classes in the same package — no import needed → Fully qualified names: java.util.ArrayList list = new java.util.ArrayList(); ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

📚 Built-In Java Packages

PackageKey Contents
java.langString, Math, Object, System, Integer, Thread — auto-imported
java.utilArrayList, HashMap, List, Collections, Arrays, Scanner, Date
java.ioFile, InputStream, OutputStream, BufferedReader, FileWriter
java.nioPath, Files, ByteBuffer — modern I/O
java.netURL, HttpURLConnection, Socket
java.sqlConnection, Statement, ResultSet, DriverManager
java.timeLocalDate, LocalDateTime, Duration, ZonedDateTime
java.util.concurrentExecutorService, Future, ConcurrentHashMap, AtomicInteger
💡 Wildcard import does NOT import sub-packages
import java.util.* gives you ArrayList, HashMap, etc. — but not java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap. You need a separate import java.util.concurrent.* for that. Each package level requires its own import.

💻 Program 1 — Declaring & Using a Package

com/example/model/Student.java 
// File must be at: src/com/example/model/Student.java
package com.example.model;           // first non-comment statement

public class Student {
    private String name;
    private int    age;

    public Student(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age  = age;
    }
    public  String getName() { return name; }
    public     int getAge()  { return age;  }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Student{" + name + ", age=" + age + "}";
    }
}
com/example/service/StudentService.java 
package com.example.service;

import com.example.model.Student;   // explicit single-type import
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class StudentService {
    private List<Student> students = new ArrayList<>();

    public void addStudent(Student s) {
        students.add(s);
    }

    public void printAll() {
        students.forEach(s ->
            System.out.println("  " + s));
    }
}
com/example/Main.java 
package com.example;

import com.example.model.Student;
import com.example.service.StudentService;

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        StudentService svc = new StudentService();
        svc.addStudent(new Student("Rahul", 20));
        svc.addStudent(new Student("Priya", 22));
        svc.addStudent(new Student("Arjun", 21));
        System.out.println("All students:");
        svc.printAll();
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
All students: Student{Rahul, age=20} Student{Priya, age=22} Student{Arjun, age=21}

💻 Program 2 — Static Import & Fully Qualified Name

ImportDemo.java 
package com.example;

// Static import — use Math constants/methods without "Math."
import static java.lang.Math.PI;
import static java.lang.Math.sqrt;
import static java.lang.Math.pow;

public class ImportDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // Static import: use PI and sqrt directly
        double radius = 7;
        double area   = PI * radius * radius;
        double hyp    = sqrt(pow(3,2) + pow(4,2));  // = 5.0

        System.out.printf("Circle area (r=7): %.4f%n", area);
        System.out.printf("Hypotenuse (3,4) : %.1f%n",  hyp);

        // Fully qualified name — no import needed but verbose
        java.util.Date today = new java.util.Date();
        System.out.println("Today: " + today.toString().substring(0,10));
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
Circle area (r=7): 153.9380 Hypotenuse (3,4) : 5.0 Today: Wed Mar 11

💻 Program 3 — Name Clash: Resolving with Fully Qualified Names

NameClash.java — two Date classes 
package com.example;

// Import one — the other must use fully qualified name
import java.sql.Date;   // sql.Date imported by simple name

public class NameClash {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // java.sql.Date — uses simple name (imported)
        Date sqlDate = new Date(System.currentTimeMillis());

        // java.util.Date — must use fully qualified name to avoid clash
        java.util.Date utilDate = new java.util.Date();

        System.out.println("sql.Date  : " + sqlDate);
        System.out.println("util.Date : " + utilDate);
        System.out.println("Same name, different packages — no conflict!");
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
sql.Date : 2026-03-11 util.Date : Wed Mar 11 16:40:43 IST 2026 Same name, different packages — no conflict!
⚠️ Wildcard import cannot resolve name clashes
If you write import java.util.*; and import java.sql.*;, both packages have a Date class. Any use of Date becomes ambiguous — compile error. The fix: import one by its simple name and use the fully qualified name for the other, as shown above.
💡 java.lang is the only auto-imported package
java.lang contains String, System, Math, Object, Integer, Thread, etc. It is automatically imported in every Java file — you never write import java.lang.*. Every other package (including java.util) requires an explicit import.
⭐ Must-Know Interview Topics
Package purpose (namespace + access) · package statement position · import types (single/wildcard/static) · java.lang auto-import · wildcard clash · fully qualified name · default package · reverse domain naming
Q1. What is a package and why is it used?
A package is a namespace that groups related Java types together, corresponding to a directory on the file system.

Two key purposes:
1. Namespace management: prevents naming collisions — two classes named Date can coexist in java.util and java.sql without conflict
2. Access control: the default (package-private) access modifier makes members visible only within the same package, providing encapsulation at the package level
Q2. Where must the package statement appear in a file?
The package statement must be the first non-comment line in the source file. Only one package statement is allowed per file. It must appear before any import statements and before the class declaration.

If there is no package statement, the class belongs to the default (unnamed) package. Classes in the default package cannot be imported by classes in named packages — so the default package is only suitable for quick experiments and single-file demos.
Q3. What is the difference between single-type import and wildcard import?
Single-type import (import java.util.ArrayList): imports exactly one class. Explicit and clear — anyone reading the file immediately sees which classes are used from which packages.

Wildcard import (import java.util.*): imports all public types in a package. Does not slow down compilation (the compiler loads only what is actually used). Does not import sub-packages. Can cause ambiguity if two packages contain a class with the same name — compile error.

Professional preference: single-type imports, enforced by Checkstyle and most IDE code style settings.
Q4. Which package is automatically imported in every Java file?
java.lang is the only package that is automatically imported in every Java source file without any explicit import statement. It contains the most fundamental classes: String, Object, System, Math, Integer, Long, Thread, Runnable, Exception, etc.

Every other package — including java.util — requires an explicit import statement.
Q5. What is a static import? When should it be used?
import static imports a static member (field or method) of a class, allowing it to be used without the class name prefix.

Example: import static java.lang.Math.PI; allows writing PI instead of Math.PI.

When to use: in test code (import static org.junit.Assert.* is idiomatic), and for frequently used constants/utility methods where the class prefix adds no clarity. When to avoid: in production code where it can obscure which class a method comes from — Math.sqrt() is clearer than a bare sqrt().
Q6. What is the default package and what are its limitations?
The default package is the unnamed package that a class belongs to when it has no package statement.

Limitations:
1. Classes in the default package cannot be imported by classes in named packages — only other default-package classes can use them
2. No way to have two classes with the same name (no namespace separation)
3. Default package classes cannot use protected package-access across sub-paths

Use only for: quick experiments, single-file compilation demos, and practice programs. Never use for any real project.
Q7. Why does Java use reverse domain names for packages?
Reverse domain naming (e.g., com.google.gson, org.apache.commons) guarantees global uniqueness of package names.

Since domain names are unique by definition (enforced by internet registrars), reversing them creates a namespace where no two organisations should produce the same package prefix — unless they control the same domain. This convention prevents collisions between libraries from different vendors even when they contain classes with identical names.

🌱 Packages in Spring Boot

Spring Boot relies heavily on its own package scanning to auto-discover beans. Understanding the package structure is critical to making component scanning work correctly.

✅ Standard Spring Boot package layout
Root package: contains @SpringBootApplication — scanning starts here
controller/: @RestController, @Controller classes
service/: @Service classes
repository/: @Repository, JPA repository interfaces
model/ or entity/: @Entity JPA classes
dto/: Data Transfer Objects
config/: @Configuration classes
exception/: custom exception classes
Spring Boot component scanning — package rules 
// com/example/ecommerce/EcommerceApplication.java
package com.example.ecommerce;    // ← ROOT package

@SpringBootApplication           // scans com.example.ecommerce and ALL sub-packages
public class EcommerceApplication {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(EcommerceApplication.class, args);
    }
}

// ✅ Discovered automatically (sub-package of root):
// package com.example.ecommerce.service;  → @Service beans found
// package com.example.ecommerce.controller; → @RestController found

// ❌ NOT discovered (different root — outside scan range):
// package com.other.service;  → beans here are NOT registered!

// To scan additional packages explicitly:
@SpringBootApplication(scanBasePackages =
    {"com.example.ecommerce", "com.shared.utils"})
public class EcommerceApplication { ... }
⚠️ @SpringBootApplication must be in the root package
If the main class is placed in the default package or in a package that is a parent of your controllers/services, Spring component scanning will either not find your beans or scan too broadly (the entire classpath). Always place the @SpringBootApplication class in the topmost package of your application, and let all other packages be sub-packages of it.
⭐ Must-Know Exam Points
  • package statement = first non-comment line in the file
  • Package maps to a directory/folder on the file system
  • java.lang = only auto-imported package
  • Wildcard import (*) does NOT import sub-packages
  • Name clash between two wildcard imports → compile error
  • Fix for name clash: import one by simple name, use fully qualified name for the other
  • Default package: no package statement → cannot be imported by named packages
  • Static import: import static ClassName.member — allows using static members without class prefix
  • Reverse domain naming → guarantees global uniqueness

📝 MCQ Traps

QuestionAnswerTrap / Reason
Which package is auto-imported?java.lang onlyjava.util, java.io etc. all need explicit import
Does import java.util.* import java.util.concurrent?NoWildcard doesn't cross into sub-packages
Two wildcard imports with same class name?Compile errorAmbiguous reference — must use fully qualified name
Can a default-package class be imported?NoNamed packages cannot import default-package classes
Where must package statement appear?First non-comment lineBefore imports and class declaration
Does wildcard import slow compilation?NoCompiler only loads actually used types — wildcard is just a convenience
What does static import do?Imports a static field/methodAllows use without class prefix; import static Math.PI → use PI directly

📖 One-Liner Definitions

  • Package — A namespace grouping related Java types, mapping to a directory; declared with the package keyword as the first statement.
  • Import — A directive that allows using a class by its simple name rather than its fully qualified name.
  • Wildcard import — import pkg.*; imports all public types from a single package level; does not include sub-packages.
  • Static import — import static pkg.Class.member; allows using a static field or method without the class name prefix.
  • Fully qualified name — The complete package + class name: java.util.ArrayList; always unique and never requires an import.
  • Default package — The unnamed package for classes with no package statement; cannot be imported by named packages.
  • Reverse domain naming — Convention of using reversed internet domain as package root (e.g., com.google.*) to ensure global uniqueness.
TOPIC 18

Access Modifiers

🔐 What are Access Modifiers?

Access modifiers control the visibility and accessibility of classes, fields, methods, and constructors. Java has four access levels — from most to least restrictive: private, default (no keyword), protected, and public.

📌 Access modifiers are the enforcement mechanism of Encapsulation
Encapsulation (Topic 7) is the principle — hide internal data, expose controlled interface. Access modifiers are the language tool that enforces that principle. Without access modifiers every field and method would be globally accessible — encapsulation would be impossible.

📊 The Access Modifier Table

ACCESS LEVELS — WHO CAN SEE WHAT ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── │ Same │ Same │ Sub- │ Other Modifier │ Class │ Package │ class │ Package ──────────────────┼───────┼─────────┼────────┼────────── private │ ✅ │ ❌ │ ❌ │ ❌ default (none) │ ✅ │ ✅ │ ❌ │ ❌ protected │ ✅ │ ✅ │ ✅ │ ❌ public │ ✅ │ ✅ │ ✅ │ ✅ ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Memory aid (most → least restrictive): private < default < protected < public

🔍 Each Modifier Explained

1. private

Accessible only within the same class. The most restrictive level. Use for all instance variables (the cornerstone of encapsulation) and for helper methods that are implementation details not meant for external callers.

PRIVATE — same class only class BankAccount { private double balance; // only BankAccount can touch this private void recalculate() {} // internal helper } class Main { BankAccount a = new BankAccount(); a.balance = 1000; // ❌ compile error — private!

2. default (package-private)

No keyword is written. Accessible to all classes in the same package. Classes in other packages (even subclasses in a different package) cannot access it. Used when you want package-internal helpers but not wider exposure.

DEFAULT — same package only (no keyword written) package com.example.service; class Helper { // default class — not public! void assist() {} // default method } // com.example.service.SomeClass → can use Helper ✅ // com.example.other.OtherClass → cannot see Helper ❌

3. protected

Accessible within the same package AND by subclasses in any package. The key nuance: a subclass in a different package can access protected members only through its own reference or subclass reference, not through a parent class reference.

PROTECTED — same package + subclasses anywhere package com.animals; class Animal { protected String name; // accessible to subclasses everywhere protected void breathe() {} } package com.pets; // different package! import com.animals.Animal; class Dog extends Animal { void show() { System.out.println(name); // ✅ own inherited field breathe(); // ✅ inherited method } }

4. public

Accessible from everywhere — any class in any package. Use for the intentional public API of your class: constructors you want others to call, methods you want others to use, and constants you want to share.

🏛️ Modifiers for Top-Level Classes

TOP-LEVEL CLASS ACCESS RULES ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Top-level classes (not nested) can only be: ✅ public → accessible from anywhere ✅ default → accessible within the same package only ❌ private class MyClass {} → compile error ❌ protected class MyClass {} → compile error One public class per .java file, and it must match the filename: File: OrderService.java → public class OrderService { } A file CAN have multiple classes, but only ONE can be public.

📐 protected — The Tricky One

PROTECTED SUBCLASS ACCESS — THE NUANCE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── package com.base; class Parent { protected int value = 42; } package com.child; class Child extends Parent { void test() { System.out.println(value); // ✅ own inherited field Child c2 = new Child(); System.out.println(c2.value); // ✅ same class (Child) Parent p = new Parent(); System.out.println(p.value); // ❌ compile error! // accessing protected via PARENT reference from different package // is NOT allowed — even in a subclass } } ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Rule: In a different package, protected access is only valid through the subclass type — not through a parent type reference.
💡 Best Practice Summary
Fields: always private — expose via getters/setters
Helper methods: private — not part of the public contract
Methods for subclasses to override/use: protected
Public API methods: public
Package utilities: default (no modifier)
Rule of thumb: use the most restrictive modifier that still allows the class to function correctly.

💻 Program 1 — All Four Modifiers in One Class

AccessDemo.java — all four modifiers 
package com.example;

public class Employee {

    public    String companyName = "TechCorp";  // anyone
    protected String department  = "Engineering"; // package + subclasses
              String teamCode    = "T42";   // package only (default)
    private   String salary      = "CONFIDENTIAL"; // this class only

    public    void introduce() {
        // can access ALL four from within the class
        System.out.println("Company : " + companyName);
        System.out.println("Dept    : " + department);
        System.out.println("Team    : " + teamCode);
        System.out.println("Salary  : " + salary);
    }

    public    void publicMethod()    { System.out.println("public method");    }
    protected void protectedMethod() { System.out.println("protected method"); }
              void defaultMethod()    { System.out.println("default method");    }
    private   void privateMethod()    { System.out.println("private method");    }
}

// Same package — can access public, protected, default; NOT private
class SamePackageTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e = new Employee();
        e.introduce();       // public — OK
        e.publicMethod();
        e.protectedMethod(); // same package — OK
        e.defaultMethod();   // same package — OK
        // e.privateMethod();  ← compile error
        System.out.println(e.companyName);  // public — OK
        System.out.println(e.teamCode);     // default — OK (same pkg)
        // System.out.println(e.salary);  ← compile error
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
Company : TechCorp Dept : Engineering Team : T42 Salary : CONFIDENTIAL public method protected method default method

💻 Program 2 — protected in Subclass (Different Package)

com/animals/Animal.java + com/pets/Dog.java 
// ── com/animals/Animal.java ──────────────────────────────────
package com.animals;
public class Animal {
    protected String name;
    protected int    age;

    public Animal(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age  = age;
    }
    protected void breathe() {
        System.out.println(name + " is breathing");
    }
}

// ── com/pets/Dog.java ─────────────────────────────────────────
package com.pets;
import com.animals.Animal;

public class Dog extends Animal {
    String breed;

    public Dog(String name, int age, String breed) {
        super(name, age);    // calls Animal constructor
        this.breed = breed;
    }

    public void describe() {
        // protected fields accessible via inheritance in subclass
        System.out.printf("%s | age:%d | breed:%s%n",
            name, age, breed);
        breathe();           // protected method — accessible in subclass

        // ❌ Would be compile error (different pkg, parent ref):
        // Animal a = new Animal("Cat",1); a.name → not accessible
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Dog("Bruno", 3, "Labrador").describe();
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
Bruno | age:3 | breed:Labrador Bruno is breathing

💻 Program 3 — Access Modifier on Constructor (Singleton + Factory)

ConstructorAccess.java 
// Private constructor = Singleton (Topic 11 revisited)
class Config {
    private static final Config INSTANCE = new Config();
    private Config() {}                  // private constructor
    public static Config getInstance() { return INSTANCE; }
    public String getDbUrl() { return "jdbc:mysql://localhost/db"; }
}

// Protected constructor — only subclasses can call new
class AbstractShape {
    String color;
    protected AbstractShape(String color) {   // protected constructor
        this.color = color;
    }
    double area() { return 0; }
}

class Square extends AbstractShape {
    double side;
    public Square(String color, double side) {
        super(color);     // can call protected parent constructor
        this.side = side;
    }
    @Override double area() { return side * side; }
}

public class ConstructorAccess {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Config cfg = Config.getInstance();
        System.out.println("DB: " + cfg.getDbUrl());
        // new Config();  ← compile error — private constructor

        Square sq = new Square("Blue", 5);
        System.out.printf("Square area: %.1f | color: %s%n",
            sq.area(), sq.color);
        // new AbstractShape("Red");  ← compile error (same pkg ok, diff pkg no)
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
DB: jdbc:mysql://localhost/db Square area: 25.0 | color: Blue
⚠️ protected ≠ "almost public"
A common misconception is that protected is nearly as open as public. In practice, the sub-package nuance makes it tricky: a subclass in a different package can access protected members only via its own inherited reference — not via a Parent type reference. This is frequently tested in exams and catches even experienced developers.
💡 Access modifier checklist for a new class
1. All fields → private (always)
2. Getters/setters → public (for external consumers) or protected (for subclasses only)
3. Template/hook methods for subclasses → protected
4. Internal utility methods → private
5. Package-scoped helpers → default (no modifier)
6. Class itself → public if used outside the package, else default
⭐ Must-Know Interview Topics
Four modifiers and their scope · access table · top-level class modifiers (public/default only) · protected nuance in different packages · override rule (same or wider) · private constructor use cases
Q1. What are the four access modifiers in Java and their visibility?
private: visible only within the same class
default (no keyword): visible to all classes in the same package
protected: visible to all classes in the same package, AND to subclasses in any package
public: visible everywhere — any class in any package

Order from most to least restrictive: private < default < protected < public
Q2. What access modifiers can be applied to a top-level class?
A top-level class (not nested) can only have two access modifiers:
public — accessible from any package
default (no keyword) — accessible only within the same package

private and protected are not valid for top-level classes — they cause a compile error. Additionally, a file can contain only one public class, and its name must match the filename exactly.
Q3. What is the exact rule for protected access in a different package?
In a different package, a subclass can access a protected member only through its own reference or an instance of its own subclass type — not through a reference of the parent class type.

Example: if Dog is in a different package from Animal and Animal.name is protected:
— this.name inside Dog → ✅ own inherited field
— new Dog().name inside Dog → ✅ same type
— new Animal().name inside Dog → ❌ compile error (parent type reference, different package)
Q4. Why should instance variables always be private?
Making instance variables private enforces encapsulation:
1. Validation: setters can validate values before assignment (if (age < 0) throw)
2. Controlled mutation: read-only fields can have getters but no setters
3. Implementation freedom: internal representation can change without breaking callers (change int age to LocalDate birthDate and update the getter — all callers still use getAge())
4. Thread safety: easier to synchronise access through controlled methods
5. Debugging: all mutations go through one point — easy to add logging/breakpoints
Q5. Can access modifier be narrowed when overriding a method?
No — narrowing is not allowed. When overriding, the access modifier must be the same or wider.

Valid: protected → public (widening ✅)
Valid: public → public (same ✅)
Invalid: public → protected (narrowing ❌ — compile error)

Reason: callers using the parent reference expect the same or more accessible contract. If a child narrowed access, code that compiled against the parent type would break at runtime — violating the Liskov Substitution Principle.
Q6. What is the difference between default and protected in terms of subclass access?
Default (package-private): accessible to all classes in the same package, but NOT accessible to subclasses in a different package. Even if Dog extends Animal and Animal has a default field, if Dog is in a different package it cannot access that field.

Protected: accessible to all classes in the same package AND to subclasses anywhere (including different packages), subject to the own-reference rule.

Summary: protected adds cross-package subclass access on top of what default provides.

🌱 Access Modifiers in Spring Boot

Spring Boot code follows strict access modifier conventions. Framework-specific annotations interact with access modifiers in important ways.

✅ Access modifier conventions in Spring Boot
@Service / @Component / @Controller — class must be public (Spring proxying requires it in most cases)
Injected fields — keep private final; use constructor injection not field injection
@RequestMapping handler methods — public (Spring MVC requires this)
@Bean methods in @Configuration — public or protected
Repository interfaces — public interface
Internal service helpers — private methods within the service class
Template methods for subclasses — protected in abstract base service
OrderService.java — access modifier best practices in Spring 
@Service
public class OrderService {                  // public class — Spring proxy

    private final OrderRepository orderRepo;  // private final — encapsulated
    private final PaymentService  paymentSvc;

    public OrderService(OrderRepository r, PaymentService p) {
        this.orderRepo  = r;
        this.paymentSvc = p;
    }

    public Order createOrder(CreateOrderRequest req) {   // public API
        validateRequest(req);        // calls private helper
        Order order = buildOrder(req);
        return orderRepo.save(order);
    }

    private void validateRequest(CreateOrderRequest req) { // private helper
        if (req == null)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Request must not be null");
    }

    private Order buildOrder(CreateOrderRequest req) {      // private builder
        return new Order(req.getProduct(), req.getQuantity());
    }
}
⚠️ Spring proxying and access modifiers
Spring uses CGLIB or JDK dynamic proxies to implement AOP features like @Transactional and @Cacheable. These proxies wrap your bean. If the method you annotate is private, Spring cannot intercept it — the annotation is silently ignored and no transaction/cache is applied. Always make @Transactional and @Cacheable methods public.
⭐ Must-Know Exam Points
  • private → same class only
  • default → same package (no keyword written)
  • protected → same package + subclasses anywhere
  • public → everywhere
  • Top-level class can only be public or default — never private/protected
  • One file → one public class, name must match filename
  • Override: access can be same or wider — never narrower
  • protected + different package: only via own/subclass reference — NOT via parent reference
  • Best practice: fields always private; expose via public getters

📝 MCQ Traps

QuestionAnswerTrap / Reason
Can a top-level class be private?No — compile errorOnly public or default allowed for top-level classes
Default access = which packages?Same package onlyNOT accessible to subclasses in different packages
Protected subclass in different package accesses via parent ref?Compile errorMust access via own/subclass ref, not parent type ref
Can override narrow access from public to protected?No — compile errorMust be same or wider access modifier
How many public classes in one .java file?One maximumMust match filename; multiple classes allowed but only one public
Can private methods be overridden?NoNot inherited — child creates a new independent method
Which is more restrictive: default or protected?defaultdefault blocks cross-package subclasses; protected allows them

📖 One-Liner Definitions

  • private — Most restrictive modifier; member accessible only within its declaring class.
  • default (package-private) — No keyword; member accessible to all classes in the same package, and no others.
  • protected — Member accessible within the same package and to subclasses in any package (via own reference).
  • public — Least restrictive; member accessible from any class in any package.
  • Access widening in override — The rule that an overriding method's access modifier must be equal to or wider than the parent method's modifier.
TOPIC 19

Polymorphism & Dynamic Method Dispatch

🎭 What is Polymorphism?

Polymorphism (Greek: "many forms") means one interface, many implementations. In Java it means a single method call can behave differently depending on the actual object type at runtime. It is one of the four pillars of OOP and is made possible by inheritance and method overriding.

📌 Two types of polymorphism
Compile-time polymorphism (Static / Early binding) → achieved via method overloading. The compiler decides which method to call based on the method signature at compile time.

Runtime polymorphism (Dynamic / Late binding) → achieved via method overriding + parent reference pointing to child object. The JVM decides which method to call at runtime based on the actual object type.

📐 Compile-Time Polymorphism — Method Overloading

COMPILE-TIME POLYMORPHISM (Overloading) ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── class Calculator { int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; } int add(int a, int b, int c){ return a+b+c; } } calc.add(2, 3) → compiler picks add(int, int) calc.add(2.0, 3.0) → compiler picks add(double, double) calc.add(1, 2, 3) → compiler picks add(int, int, int) Resolution at COMPILE TIME → "early binding" Reference type determines which method → no runtime surprise

🔄 Runtime Polymorphism — Dynamic Method Dispatch

Dynamic Method Dispatch (DMD) is the mechanism by which Java selects the correct overridden method to call at runtime, based on the actual object type — not the declared reference type.

RUNTIME POLYMORPHISM — THE KEY PATTERN ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Parent reference → Child object ↓ Method call resolved by actual object type at RUNTIME Animal a; // reference type: Animal a = new Dog(); // actual object: Dog a.sound(); // → calls Dog.sound() ← DMD a = new Cat(); // reassign to Cat object a.sound(); // → calls Cat.sound() ← DMD Same reference variable `a`, different objects, different behaviour! This is the power of polymorphism.

🧠 How Dynamic Method Dispatch Works (vtable)

JVM VTABLE MECHANISM ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Every class has a virtual method table (vtable): Animal vtable: Dog vtable: ┌─────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ sound() → A.sound│ │ sound() → D.sound │ ← overridden │ eat() → A.eat │ │ eat() → A.eat │ ← inherited │ toString→ O.toStr│ │ toString→ O.toStr │ └─────────────────┘ └──────────────────────┘ Animal a = new Dog(); a.sound() │ ├─ compiler: "sound() is defined in Animal — OK to compile" │ uses Animal reference type for type checking │ └─ JVM at runtime: "actual object is Dog" "look up Dog.vtable[sound()]" "call Dog.sound()" The vtable lookup is what makes polymorphism work.

⚡ What Participates in Runtime Polymorphism

Runtime Polymorphism?Reason
Instance methods (overridden)✅ YESResolved via vtable at runtime
Static methods❌ NOResolved by reference type at compile time (method hiding)
Instance variables (fields)❌ NOField access resolved by reference type, not object type
Constructors❌ NOConstructors are not inherited or overridden
Private methods❌ NONot inherited — no vtable entry in child
final methods❌ NOCannot be overridden — single vtable entry

🌊 The Power: Polymorphic Arrays & Collections

POLYMORPHISM WITH ARRAYS — process many types uniformly ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Shape[] shapes = { new Circle(5), new Rectangle(4, 6), new Triangle(3, 8) }; for (Shape s : shapes) s.area(); // each calls ITS OWN area() implementation // no if/else, no instanceof needed! This is the open/closed principle: → Open for extension (add new Shape subclasses freely) → Closed for modification (the loop never changes) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

🔍 Field Hiding — The Non-Polymorphic Trap

INSTANCE VARIABLES DO NOT PARTICIPATE IN POLYMORPHISM ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── class Animal { String type = "Animal"; } class Dog extends Animal { String type = "Dog"; } Animal a = new Dog(); System.out.println(a.type); // prints "Animal" ← reference type! // NOT "Dog" — fields use early binding Dog d = new Dog(); System.out.println(d.type); // prints "Dog" Rule: field access is always resolved by the REFERENCE type, method calls are resolved by the OBJECT type (if overridden).

💻 Program 1 — Runtime Polymorphism: Shape Calculator

Polymorphism.java 
abstract class Shape {
    String color;
    Shape(String color) { this.color = color; }
    abstract double area();              // must override
    abstract double perimeter();         // must override
    void printInfo() {                   // calls overridden methods via DMD
        System.out.printf("%-12s color:%-6s area:%7.2f  perim:%7.2f%n",
            getClass().getSimpleName(), color, area(), perimeter());
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    double r;
    Circle(String c, double r) { super(c); this.r = r; }
    @Override double area()      { return Math.PI * r * r; }
    @Override double perimeter() { return 2 * Math.PI * r; }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double w, h;
    Rectangle(String c, double w, double h) {
        super(c); this.w = w; this.h = h;
    }
    @Override double area()      { return w * h; }
    @Override double perimeter() { return 2 * (w + h); }
}

class Triangle extends Shape {
    double a, b, c;
    Triangle(String col, double a, double b, double c) {
        super(col); this.a=a; this.b=b; this.c=c;
    }
    @Override double area() {
        double s = (a+b+c)/2;
        return Math.sqrt(s*(s-a)*(s-b)*(s-c));
    }
    @Override double perimeter() { return a+b+c; }
}

public class Polymorphism {
    static void printTotal(Shape[] shapes) {
        double total = 0;
        for (Shape s : shapes) {
            s.printInfo();   // DMD: each shape prints its own info
            total += s.area();
        }
        System.out.printf("Total area: %.2f%n", total);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape[] shapes = {
            new Circle("Red",    5),
            new Rectangle("Blue",  4, 6),
            new Triangle("Green", 3, 4, 5)
        };
        printTotal(shapes);
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
Circle color:Red area: 78.54 perim: 31.42 Rectangle color:Blue area: 24.00 perim: 20.00 Triangle color:Green area: 6.00 perim: 12.00 Total area: 108.54

💻 Program 2 — Field Hiding vs Method Overriding

FieldVsMethod.java 
class Base {
    String type = "Base";                          // instance field
    void   show() { System.out.println("Base.show()"); }
}

class Derived extends Base {
    String type = "Derived";                       // hides Base.type
    @Override
    void   show() { System.out.println("Derived.show()"); }
}

public class FieldVsMethod {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Base ref = new Derived();  // parent ref, child object

        // Method → RUNTIME resolution (actual object type)
        ref.show();                 // "Derived.show()" ← DMD

        // Field → COMPILE-TIME resolution (reference type)
        System.out.println(ref.type);  // "Base" ← reference type wins!

        // Cast to Derived to access Derived.type
        Derived d = (Derived) ref;
        System.out.println(d.type);     // "Derived"
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
Derived.show() Base Derived

💻 Program 3 — Compile-Time Polymorphism: Overloading Resolution

CompileTimePolymorphism.java 
public class Printer {

    void print(int n) {
        System.out.println("int:    " + n);
    }
    void print(double d) {
        System.out.println("double: " + d);
    }
    void print(String s) {
        System.out.println("String: " + s);
    }
    void print(int a, int b) {
        System.out.println("int,int: " + a + "," + b);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Printer p = new Printer();
        p.print(42);         // → print(int)
        p.print(3.14);       // → print(double)
        p.print("Hello");    // → print(String)
        p.print(10, 20);     // → print(int, int)
        p.print('A');        // → print(int) — char promoted to int!
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
int: 42 double: 3.14 String: Hello int,int: 10,20 int: 65
⚠️ Fields are NOT polymorphic — a common exam trap
When you access a field via a parent reference pointing to a child object, you get the parent's field value — not the child's. Field access is resolved at compile time by the reference type. Only method calls go through dynamic dispatch. This is why exposing fields directly (without getters) breaks polymorphism.
💡 The real-world power: Open/Closed Principle
Polymorphism enables the Open/Closed Principle (OCP): code is open for extension (add new Shape subclasses) but closed for modification (the printTotal(Shape[]) method never needs to change). This is the foundation of scalable, maintainable software design.
⭐ Must-Know Interview Topics
Two types of polymorphism · Dynamic Method Dispatch definition · vtable mechanism · what participates in DMD (not fields, not static, not private) · field hiding trap · Open/Closed principle connection · parent reference + child object pattern
Q1. What is polymorphism? What are its two types in Java?
Polymorphism means "many forms" — the ability of a single method call to behave differently depending on the object it is called on.

Compile-time polymorphism (static/early binding): achieved via method overloading. The compiler resolves the method call at compile time based on the method signature. The reference type determines which overloaded method runs.

Runtime polymorphism (dynamic/late binding): achieved via method overriding + parent reference pointing to child object. The JVM resolves the method call at runtime based on the actual object type. This is Dynamic Method Dispatch.
Q2. What is Dynamic Method Dispatch?
Dynamic Method Dispatch (DMD) is the JVM mechanism that resolves an overridden instance method call at runtime based on the actual type of the object on the Heap, not the declared type of the reference variable.

The JVM uses a virtual method table (vtable) — a per-class table mapping method names to their most-derived implementation. When a method is called, the JVM follows: reference → object → object's class → vtable → call the correct override.

This is what makes Animal a = new Dog(); a.sound(); call Dog.sound() instead of Animal.sound().
Q3. What does NOT participate in runtime polymorphism?
Static methods — resolved by reference type at compile time (method hiding)
Instance variables (fields) — also resolved by reference type (field hiding)
Private methods — not inherited, so no vtable entry in child
final methods — cannot be overridden, single vtable entry
Constructors — never inherited or overridden

Only non-static, non-private, non-final instance methods that are overridden participate in DMD.
Q4. Can you achieve runtime polymorphism without inheritance?
Not with classes alone — you need a supertype (class or interface) that both the reference type and the implementing type share. Runtime polymorphism requires:
1. A parent reference (or interface reference)
2. A child/implementing object assigned to it
3. The method overridden in the child

However, with interfaces you don't need class inheritance — a class can implement an interface and be referenced by that interface type, achieving full runtime polymorphism without extending any class. This is actually the preferred form in modern Java and Spring Boot.
Q5. What is the difference between early binding and late binding?
Early binding (compile-time / static binding): the method to call is determined at compile time by the compiler. Applies to: overloaded methods, static methods, private methods, final methods. The binding is "early" because it happens before runtime.

Late binding (runtime / dynamic binding): the method to call is determined at runtime by the JVM based on the actual object type. Applies to: overridden instance methods. The binding is "late" because it is deferred until the program is running.

Dynamic Method Dispatch is Java's implementation of late binding.
Q6. Explain: Animal a = new Dog(); What can you access via `a`?
Via reference a of type Animal:
You CAN access: all members declared in Animal (fields and methods). The compiler uses the reference type (Animal) to check what is accessible.
You CANNOT access: members declared only in Dog (e.g., a.bark() → compile error — bark() is not in Animal).

At runtime, if you call a.sound() and Dog overrides sound(), the JVM calls Dog's version — DMD.
If you call a.type (a field), you get Animal's field value — no DMD for fields.
To access Dog-specific members, you must downcast: ((Dog)a).bark().
Q7. How does polymorphism relate to the Open/Closed Principle?
The Open/Closed Principle (OCP) states that software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification.

Polymorphism enables this: a method that accepts a Shape parameter works with any current and future Shape subclass without modification. Adding a Pentagon class requires zero changes to the calling code — just write Pentagon and pass it in. The loop for (Shape s : shapes) s.area() works forever without touching it.

Without polymorphism you would need if (shape instanceof Circle) ... else if (shape instanceof Rectangle)... — and every new shape requires editing that code. Polymorphism eliminates those conditionals.

🌱 Polymorphism in Spring Boot

Spring Boot is built on polymorphism. Every bean injection, service interface, and repository relies on runtime polymorphism to swap implementations without changing calling code.

✅ Polymorphism patterns in Spring Boot
Interface injection — inject by interface type; Spring resolves to the concrete bean at runtime
Multiple implementations — inject a List<PaymentService> to get all implementations
@Primary / @Qualifier — choose which concrete bean to inject when multiple exist
Strategy pattern via Spring — pick payment processor at runtime based on order type
Repository interfaces — JpaRepository is polymorphically resolved to SimpleJpaRepository at runtime
PaymentService interface + implementations — Spring polymorphism 
// Interface — the polymorphic supertype
public interface PaymentService {
    void processPayment(double amount);
    String getType();
}

// Concrete implementations
@Service class UpiPaymentService implements PaymentService {
    public void processPayment(double amount) {
        System.out.println("UPI: paying " + amount);
    }
    public String getType() { return "UPI"; }
}

@Service class CardPaymentService implements PaymentService {
    public void processPayment(double amount) {
        System.out.println("Card: paying " + amount);
    }
    public String getType() { return "CARD"; }
}

// OrderService — inject ALL PaymentService implementations
@Service
public class OrderService {
    private final List<PaymentService> paymentServices;

    public OrderService(List<PaymentService> services) {
        this.paymentServices = services;
    }

    public void pay(String type, double amount) {
        paymentServices.stream()
            .filter(s -> s.getType().equalsIgnoreCase(type))
            .findFirst()
            .ifPresent(s -> s.processPayment(amount));
            // DMD: calls the right processPayment() at runtime
    }
}
📌 Spring IoC is polymorphism at the framework level
Spring's dependency injection is polymorphism applied at the container level. When you declare private final PaymentService paymentSvc, Spring decides at runtime which concrete bean to inject — UpiPaymentService or CardPaymentService. The calling class never needs to know which implementation it got. This is the Strategy pattern powered by runtime polymorphism.
⭐ Must-Know Exam Points
  • Compile-time polymorphism → method overloading → resolved by reference type at compile time
  • Runtime polymorphism → method overriding → resolved by object type at runtime
  • Dynamic Method Dispatch = JVM mechanism for runtime polymorphism via vtable
  • Fields (instance variables) use early binding — NOT polymorphic
  • Static methods use early binding — NOT polymorphic (method hiding)
  • private, final methods → NOT polymorphic
  • Pattern: Parent ref = new Child() — method call uses Child's override
  • Cannot call Child-specific methods via Parent reference (compile error)
  • Downcast needed: ((Child)ref).childMethod()

📝 MCQ Traps

Code / QuestionOutput / AnswerTrap / Reason
Animal a=new Dog(); a.sound() where Dog overrides sound()Dog's sound()Runtime polymorphism — object type wins
Animal a=new Dog(); a.type where both have field typeAnimal's type valueFields use reference type — no DMD for fields
Animal a=new Dog(); a.staticMethod()Animal's static methodStatic uses reference type — method hiding, not overriding
Overloading is which type of polymorphism?Compile-timeResolved at compile time by signature — not runtime
Can private method be polymorphic?NoPrivate not inherited — child's same-name method is a NEW method
What resolves which overloaded method to call?Argument types at compile timeThe compiler picks based on declared argument types, not runtime types

📖 One-Liner Definitions

  • Polymorphism — The ability of one interface/method to take many forms; implemented via overloading (compile-time) and overriding (runtime) in Java.
  • Dynamic Method Dispatch — JVM mechanism that resolves overridden method calls at runtime based on the actual object type, using a virtual method table (vtable).
  • Early binding — Method resolution at compile time; applies to overloaded, static, private, and final methods.
  • Late binding — Method resolution at runtime; applies to overridden instance methods; implemented via vtable lookup.
  • vtable (virtual method table) — A per-class table maintained by the JVM mapping method names to their most-derived implementations; the mechanism behind DMD.
  • Field hiding — When a child class declares a field with the same name as the parent; resolved by reference type (not runtime type) — does not participate in polymorphism.
TOPIC 20

final Keyword

🔒 The final Keyword — Three Contexts

The final keyword means "cannot be changed after this point." It applies in three completely different contexts — variable, method, and class — and the meaning is analogous in each: lock it down.

THREE USES OF final ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1. final variable → value cannot be reassigned after init 2. final method → cannot be overridden by any subclass 3. final class → cannot be extended (no subclasses allowed) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1️⃣ final Variable

A final variable can be assigned only once. After the first assignment it becomes read-only. This applies to local variables, method parameters, instance fields, and static fields.

FINAL VARIABLE RULES ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── A) final local variable — must be assigned before use: final int x = 10; x = 20; // ❌ compile error B) final instance field — must be assigned in: → Declaration: final int MAX = 100; → Constructor (any): this.MAX = 100; inside constructor → Instance init block: { MAX = 100; } NOT in a regular method after construction! C) final static field (constant): static final double PI = 3.14159; → Assigned at declaration or in static initialiser block → Convention: ALL_CAPS_WITH_UNDERSCORES D) final reference variable: final List list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add("hello"); // ✅ — list content CAN change list = new ArrayList<>(); // ❌ — reference itself cannot change final = makes the REFERENCE constant, not the OBJECT!
⚠️ final reference ≠ immutable object
A final reference variable means you cannot reassign the variable to point to a different object. But the object itself can still be mutated through its own methods. To make a truly immutable object, you need the class itself to be designed for immutability (private fields, no setters, final class — like String).

2️⃣ final Method

A final method cannot be overridden by any subclass. The implementation is locked. Useful when a method's behaviour must not change in subclasses for correctness or security reasons.

FINAL METHOD ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── class Account { final void debit(double amount) { // locked — cannot be changed if (amount <= 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException(); balance -= amount; } void getBalance() { ... } // can be overridden } class SavingsAccount extends Account { // @Override void debit(...) { } // ❌ compile error! @Override void getBalance() { ... } // ✅ non-final — OK } Use case: security-critical logic, template method pattern where the skeleton must not change, factory creation steps.

3️⃣ final Class

A final class cannot be extended. No class can declare extends FinalClass. This is the strongest form of locking — the class design is frozen completely.

FINAL CLASS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── public final class String { ... } // JDK — cannot be subclassed public final class Integer { ... } // JDK wrapper — same public final class Math { ... } // JDK — same class MyString extends String { } // ❌ compile error! Why make a class final? → Security: prevent malicious subclasses from overriding methods → Immutability: ensures no subclass can add mutable state → Performance: JVM can inline final class method calls (devirtualise) → Design: the class is intentionally complete — extension is wrong Note: all methods in a final class are implicitly final too. You cannot instantiate a final class differently — just cannot extend it.

🔗 final + static = Constant

CONSTANTS — static final ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── public class MathConstants { public static final double PI = 3.14159265358979; public static final int MAX_AGE = 150; public static final String APP_NAME = "MyApp"; } Access: MathConstants.PI // class name dot constant name MathConstants.MAX_AGE Convention: → ALL_CAPS_WITH_UNDERSCORES for constant names → public static final for shared constants → Compile-time constants (primitives + String) are inlined by compiler into every usage site
💡 Prefer final everywhere you can
Using final on local variables, fields, and parameters wherever possible:
— Makes code easier to reason about (value won't change unexpectedly)
— Enables compiler and JVM optimisations
— Catches accidental reassignment bugs at compile time
— Signals intent to other developers: "this is not meant to change"
Modern Java style: constructor-injected Spring beans are private final fields — immutable references.

💻 Program 1 — final Variable in All Contexts

FinalVariable.java 
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class FinalVariable {

    // C) static final constant — class-level, shared, immutable ref
    public static final double TAX_RATE  = 0.18;
    public static final String APP_NAME  = "ShopApp";

    // B) final instance field — must be set in constructor
    private final String orderId;
    private final double basePrice;

    public FinalVariable(String orderId, double basePrice) {
        this.orderId   = orderId;    // assigned once here
        this.basePrice = basePrice;  // assigned once here
    }

    public void processOrder() {
        // A) final local variable
        final double tax   = basePrice * TAX_RATE;
        final double total = basePrice + tax;
        // tax = 0;  ← compile error: cannot assign to final

        System.out.printf("[%s] App: %s%n", orderId, APP_NAME);
        System.out.printf("Base: %.2f | Tax(%.0f%%): %.2f | Total: %.2f%n",
            basePrice, TAX_RATE * 100, tax, total);
    }

    public static void demonstrateFinalRef() {
        // D) final reference — reference locked, object mutable
        final List<String> items = new ArrayList<>();
        items.add("Apple");   // ✅ mutating the object is fine
        items.add("Mango");
        System.out.println("Items: " + items);
        // items = new ArrayList<>();  ← compile error: final ref!
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new FinalVariable("ORD-001", 500).processOrder();
        demonstrateFinalRef();
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
[ORD-001] App: ShopApp Base: 500.00 | Tax(18%): 90.00 | Total: 590.00 Items: [Apple, Mango]

💻 Program 2 — final Method & final Class

FinalMethodClass.java 
class Payment {
    protected double amount;

    Payment(double amount) { this.amount = amount; }

    // final method — security-critical; no subclass can change this logic
    final void applyGST() {
        amount *= 1.18;
        System.out.printf("GST applied. New amount: %.2f%n", amount);
    }

    // non-final — subclasses can customise
    void processPayment() {
        System.out.println("Generic payment: " + amount);
    }
}

class UpiPayment extends Payment {
    UpiPayment(double amount) { super(amount); }

    // ❌ Cannot override final method:
    // void applyGST() { }   → compile error

    @Override
    void processPayment() {       // ✅ can override non-final
        System.out.printf("UPI payment via VPA: %.2f%n", amount);
    }
}

// final class — cannot be subclassed
final class SSLCertificate {
    private final String issuer;
    private final String subject;

    SSLCertificate(String issuer, String subject) {
        this.issuer  = issuer;
        this.subject = subject;
    }
    void display() {
        System.out.printf("Cert — Issuer: %s | Subject: %s%n",
            issuer, subject);
    }
}
// class HackedCert extends SSLCertificate {}  ← compile error!

public class FinalMethodClass {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        UpiPayment upi = new UpiPayment(1000);
        upi.applyGST();        // calls Payment.applyGST() — locked
        upi.processPayment();  // calls UpiPayment.processPayment() — overridden

        new SSLCertificate("DigiCert", "example.com").display();
    }
}
▶ OUTPUT
